main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Practical ϵ-Exploring Thompson Sampling (PETS)","Langevin Monte Carlo (LMC) for posterior sampling","Maintaining n parallel Markov chains for diverse posterior samples","Gradient-based optimization for action selection","Gradient-free optimization for action selection"],"problems":["Efficient exploration in reinforcement learning with high-dimensional continuous controls","Intractability of posterior over action-value function parameters in RL","Correlated posterior samples from naive LMC leading to poor exploration","Difficulty of finding optimal actions in continuous action spaces after posterior sampling"],"datasets":["Benchmark continuous control tasks (specific environments not named in the provided text)"],"metrics":["Regret","Performance improvement (e.g., return)","Stability of RL algorithms"],"results":[{"metric":"Regret bound for TS with gradient-based optimization","value":"Matches best-known bound of O(d^{3/2} H^{3/2} sqrt(T)) from discrete setting"},{"metric":"Performance improvement","value":"PETS notably improves results and stability of POMP, MBPO, and SAC on benchmark continuous control tasks"}],"novelty_claims":["First practical Thompson Sampling-based exploration technique for RL with continuous controls using LMC and parallel Markov chains.","Theoretical regret analysis for TS in RL with continuous controls, matching best-known discrete setting bounds.","Demonstration that PETS can be integrated into existing RL algorithms (POMP, MBPO, SAC) without modifying their core algorithms or hyperparameters.","Empirical evidence that maintaining n parallel Markov chains for posterior sampling improves exploration diversity and performance."]}

methods: Practical ϵ-Exploring Thompson Sampling (PETS)
Langevin Monte Carlo (LMC) for posterior sampling
Maintaining n parallel Markov chains for diverse posterior samples
Gradient-based optimization for action selection
Gradient-free optimization for action selection
problems: Efficient exploration in reinforcement learning with high-dimensional continuous controls
Intractability of posterior over action-value function parameters in RL
Correlated posterior samples from naive LMC leading to poor exploration
Difficulty of finding optimal actions in continuous action spaces after posterior sampling
datasets: Benchmark continuous control tasks (specific environments not named in the provided text)

results.metric: Regret bound for TS with gradient-based optimization
results.value: Matches best-known bound of O(d^{3/2} H^{3/2} sqrt(T)) from discrete setting
PETS notably improves results and stability of POMP, MBPO, and SAC on benchmark continuous control tasks

novelty_claims: First practical Thompson Sampling-based exploration technique for RL with continuous controls using LMC and parallel Markov chains.
Theoretical regret analysis for TS in RL with continuous controls, matching best-known discrete setting bounds.
Demonstration that PETS can be integrated into existing RL algorithms (POMP, MBPO, SAC) without modifying their core algorithms or hyperparameters.
Empirical evidence that maintaining n parallel Markov chains for posterior sampling improves exploration diversity and performance.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Langevin Monte Carlo Least-Squares Value Iteration (LMC-LSVI)","Adam Langevin Monte Carlo Deep Q-Network (Adam LMCDQN)"],"problems":["Efficient exploration in reinforcement learning (RL)","Bridging the gap between provably efficient RL algorithms and practical deep RL exploration methods","Overcoming limitations of Gaussian posterior approximations in Thompson sampling for RL"],"datasets":["RiverSwim environment","Simulated linear MDPs","N-chain environment","Atari57 suite (Atari environments)"],"metrics":["Regret (in linear MDPs)","Game score (in Atari environments)"],"results":[{"metric":"Regret (linear MDPs)","value":"\\tilde{O}(d^{3/2}H^{3/2}\\sqrt{T})"},{"metric":"Performance on Atari57","value":"Better or similar results compared with state-of-the-art deep RL algorithms"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a practical and efficient exploration strategy for RL based on directly sampling the Q function posterior using Langevin Monte Carlo, rather than Gaussian approximations.","Introduces LMC-LSVI, which achieves the best known dependency on feature dimension and planning horizon in regret bounds for randomized algorithms in linear MDPs.","Demonstrates that the method is easily extendable to deep RL via Adam LMCDQN, unifying theoretical guarantees and practical performance.","Shows that the approach is scalable, easy to implement, and effective in high-dimensional RL tasks, including image-based control.","Provides both rigorous theoretical analysis and empirical results, which is rare in the literature."]}

methods: Langevin Monte Carlo Least-Squares Value Iteration (LMC-LSVI)
Adam Langevin Monte Carlo Deep Q-Network (Adam LMCDQN)
problems: Efficient exploration in reinforcement learning (RL)
Bridging the gap between provably efficient RL algorithms and practical deep RL exploration methods
Overcoming limitations of Gaussian posterior approximations in Thompson sampling for RL

results.value: Better or similar results compared with state-of-the-art deep RL algorithms

novelty_claims: Proposes a practical and efficient exploration strategy for RL based on directly sampling the Q function posterior using Langevin Monte Carlo, rather than Gaussian approximations.
Introduces LMC-LSVI, which achieves the best known dependency on feature dimension and planning horizon in regret bounds for randomized algorithms in linear MDPs.
Demonstrates that the method is easily extendable to deep RL via Adam LMCDQN, unifying theoretical guarantees and practical performance.
Shows that the approach is scalable, easy to implement, and effective in high-dimensional RL tasks, including image-based control.
Provides both rigorous theoretical analysis and empirical results, which is rare in the literature.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Neural Thompson Sampling (NeuralTS): a Thompson Sampling algorithm using deep neural networks, where the posterior mean is given by the neural network and the variance is based on neural tangent features"],"problems":["Contextual multi-armed bandit problem: optimizing the trade-off between exploration and exploitation in sequential decision making, especially with complex (nonlinear) reward functions"],"datasets":["Various benchmark bandit datasets (exact names not specified in the provided text)"],"metrics":["Cumulative regret"],"results":[{"metric":"Cumulative regret upper bound","value":"O(T^{1/2})"},{"metric":"Empirical performance","value":"Competitive with state-of-the-art baselines (exact numbers not specified in the provided text)"}],"novelty_claims":["First near-optimal regret bound for neural network-based Thompson Sampling in contextual bandits","NeuralTS considers weight uncertainty in all layers, unlike NeuralLinear which only considers the last layer","NeuralTS samples the estimated reward from the posterior, as opposed to sampling parameters as in other neural network-based TS implementations","Provides both strong theoretical guarantees (regret bound) and empirical validation"]}

methods: Neural Thompson Sampling (NeuralTS): a Thompson Sampling algorithm using deep neural networks, where the posterior mean is given by the neural network and the variance is based on neural tangent features
problems: Contextual multi-armed bandit problem: optimizing the trade-off between exploration and exploitation in sequential decision making, especially with complex (nonlinear) reward functions
datasets: Various benchmark bandit datasets (exact names not specified in the provided text)

results.value: Competitive with state-of-the-art baselines (exact numbers not specified in the provided text)

novelty_claims: First near-optimal regret bound for neural network-based Thompson Sampling in contextual bandits
NeuralTS considers weight uncertainty in all layers, unlike NeuralLinear which only considers the last layer
NeuralTS samples the estimated reward from the posterior, as opposed to sampling parameters as in other neural network-based TS implementations
Provides both strong theoretical guarantees (regret bound) and empirical validation

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Langevin Monte Carlo Thompson Sampling (LMC-TS)","Direct posterior sampling using Langevin Monte Carlo (LMC) in contextual bandits"],"problems":["Efficient posterior sampling in contextual bandits, especially in high-dimensional settings","Overcoming inefficiency and inaccuracy of Laplace (Gaussian) approximation in Thompson sampling for general reward functions","Scalability of Thompson sampling to large-scale and deep learning-based contextual bandit problems"],"datasets":["Synthetic datasets","UCI machine learning datasets","CIFAR10 image dataset"],"metrics":["Regret (cumulative regret)"],"results":[{"metric":"Regret bound for linear contextual bandits","value":"\\widetilde{O}(d\\sqrt{dT})"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes LMC-TS, a practical and efficient bandit algorithm that uses noisy gradient descent (Langevin Monte Carlo) to sample from the true posterior, avoiding Laplace approximation.","LMC-TS is scalable to high-dimensional and large-scale problems, including those involving deep neural networks.","LMC-TS is applicable to a wide range of contextual bandit models: linear, generalized linear, and neural contextual bandits.","Theoretical guarantee: LMC-TS achieves the best known sublinear regret bound for linear contextual bandits, matching state-of-the-art Thompson sampling algorithms.","Empirical demonstration that a single algorithm (LMC-TS) can handle various complex bandit models and is competitive in performance."]}

methods: Langevin Monte Carlo Thompson Sampling (LMC-TS)
Direct posterior sampling using Langevin Monte Carlo (LMC) in contextual bandits
problems: Efficient posterior sampling in contextual bandits, especially in high-dimensional settings
Overcoming inefficiency and inaccuracy of Laplace (Gaussian) approximation in Thompson sampling for general reward functions
Scalability of Thompson sampling to large-scale and deep learning-based contextual bandit problems

results.metric: Regret bound for linear contextual bandits
results.value: \widetilde{O}(d\sqrt{dT})

novelty_claims: Proposes LMC-TS, a practical and efficient bandit algorithm that uses noisy gradient descent (Langevin Monte Carlo) to sample from the true posterior, avoiding Laplace approximation.
LMC-TS is scalable to high-dimensional and large-scale problems, including those involving deep neural networks.
LMC-TS is applicable to a wide range of contextual bandit models: linear, generalized linear, and neural contextual bandits.
Theoretical guarantee: LMC-TS achieves the best known sublinear regret bound for linear contextual bandits, matching state-of-the-art Thompson sampling algorithms.
Empirical demonstration that a single algorithm (LMC-TS) can handle various complex bandit models and is competitive in performance.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Epsilon-greedy Thompson Sampling (ε-greedy TS) for Bayesian Optimization","Generic Thompson Sampling (TS)","Sample-average Thompson Sampling (TS)"],"problems":["Improving the exploitation capability of Thompson Sampling in Bayesian Optimization","Balancing the exploration-exploitation trade-off in Bayesian Optimization, especially for black-box, expensive objective functions"],"datasets":["Two benchmark functions (not named in the excerpt)","Inverse problem of a steel cantilever beam"],"metrics":["Robustness (empirical performance across problems)","Objective function value (minimization)"],"results":[{"metric":"Robustness","value":"ε-greedy TS is more robust than both generic TS and sample-average TS, matching or outperforming the better of the two across various problems"}],"novelty_claims":["First application and empirical evaluation of ε-greedy Thompson Sampling in Bayesian Optimization","Proposes a simple and effective method (ε-greedy TS) that improves exploitation in TS for BO by randomly switching between generic TS (exploration) and sample-average TS (exploitation) based on ε parameter","Demonstrates that ε-greedy TS with an appropriate ε is more robust than either extreme (pure exploration or pure exploitation)"]}

OPPO attains the same regret bound even with adversarially chosen reward functions, unlike previous value-iteration-based algorithms
OPPO does not require knowledge of transition dynamics or access to a simulator

Introduces an exploratory formulation for state dynamics that captures repetitive learning under exploration in continuous time

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["FGTS.CDB: Feel-Good Thompson Sampling for linear contextual dueling bandits, with a new exploration term tailored for dueling bandits"],"problems":["Efficient exploration and learning in linear contextual dueling bandits, especially with large or infinite action spaces","Achieving minimax-optimal regret in contextual dueling bandits using posterior sampling methods"],"datasets":["Synthetic data (used for evaluation)"],"metrics":["Regret (cumulative regret over T rounds)"],"results":[{"metric":"Regret bound","value":"O(d√T) (minimax-optimal, up to logarithmic factors)"},{"metric":"Empirical performance","value":"FGTS.CDB outperforms MaxInP, MaxPairUCB, CoLSTIM, and VACDB by a large margin on synthetic data"}],"novelty_claims":["First Thompson sampling-based algorithm (FGTS.CDB) for linear contextual dueling bandits","Introduction of a new Feel-Good exploration term specifically designed for dueling bandits, leveraging independence of two arms and avoiding cross terms","FGTS.CDB achieves nearly minimax-optimal regret (O(d√T)), matching the best known UCB-based algorithms","FGTS.CDB can efficiently handle infinite action spaces, unlike most prior methods","Theoretical analysis extends to general reward functions and recovers regret bounds for several important cases"]}

methods: FGTS.CDB: Feel-Good Thompson Sampling for linear contextual dueling bandits, with a new exploration term tailored for dueling bandits
problems: Efficient exploration and learning in linear contextual dueling bandits, especially with large or infinite action spaces

results.value: O(d√T) (minimax-optimal, up to logarithmic factors)

novelty_claims: First Thompson sampling-based algorithm (FGTS.CDB) for linear contextual dueling bandits

Use of simple hash functions to map states to hash codes for counting

Computation of reward bonus based on state visitation counts via hash table

novelty_claims: A simple generalization of classic count-based exploration using hash functions enables effective exploration in high-dimensional and continuous state spaces.

The approach is complementary to most existing RL algorithms and works across different domains.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Coin Flip Network (CFN): a neural network trained to predict the average of Rademacher (coin flip) samples to estimate state visitation counts reinforcement learning algorithms"],"problems":["Estimating pseudocounts for count-based exploration in high-dimensional or large state spaces where true counts are unavailable","Overcoming the sparse-reward and highly stochastic environments"],"datasets":["Visual Gridworld (Allen et al., 2021)","Visual TAXI (Dietterich, 1998)","Sparse-reward continuous control problems (not named specifically)","Atari game Montezuma's Revenge (Bellemare et al., 2013)"],"metrics":["Accuracy of ground-truth visitation count estimation","Reinforcement learning performance (e.g., score or reward) on benchmark tasks","Robustness to transition noise"],"results":[{"metric":"Ground-truth count recovery (Gridworld, TAXI)","value":"CFN recovers ground-truth counts while other pseudocount methods do not"},{"metric":"RL performance (sparse-reward continuous control)","value":"CFN outperforms baseline actor-critic and random network distillation, especially in challenging exploration domains"},{"metric":"RL performance (Montezuma's Revenge)","value":"CFN outperforms baseline Rainbow and is competitive with state-of-the-art exploration algorithms"},{"metric":"Robustness to transition noise (Gridworld, Montezuma's Revenge)","value":"CFN degrades less rapidly than RND as transition noise increases"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a new method for estimating pseudocounts using Rademacher (coin flip) samples, avoiding the restrictive requirements of density-based methods","Introduces the Coin Flip Network (CFN), which can be trained with any function approximator and exploration bonuses lead to superior or competitive performance on challenging RL benchmarks, with improved robustness to stochasticity"]}

methods: Coin Flip Network (CFN): a neural network trained to predict the average of Rademacher (coin flip) samples to estimate state visitation counts (pseudocounts)
Supervised learning objective to predict the inverse of a state's visitation count from coin flip samples
Use of CFN-derived pseudocounts as an exploration bonus in model-free reinforcement learning algorithms
problems: Estimating pseudocounts for count-based exploration in high-dimensional or large state spaces where true counts are unavailable
Overcoming the limitations of density-based pseudocount estimation methods, such as architectural restrictions and sensitivity to hyperparameters
Improving exploration in reinforcement learning, especially in sparse-reward and highly stochastic environments
datasets: Sparse-reward continuous control problems (not named specifically)
Atari game Montezuma's Revenge (Bellemare et al., 2013)
metrics: Accuracy of ground-truth visitation count estimation
Reinforcement learning performance (e.g., score or reward) on benchmark tasks

results.value: CFN recovers ground-truth counts while other pseudocount methods do not
CFN outperforms baseline actor-critic and random network distillation, especially in challenging exploration domains
CFN outperforms baseline Rainbow and is competitive with state-of-the-art exploration algorithms

results.metric: Robustness to transition noise (Gridworld, Montezuma's Revenge)
results.value: CFN degrades less rapidly than RND as transition noise increases

novelty_claims: Proposes a new method for estimating pseudocounts using Rademacher (coin flip) samples, avoiding the restrictive requirements of density-based methods
Introduces the Coin Flip Network (CFN), which can be trained with any function approximator and is not sensitive to network architecture or training procedure
Demonstrates that CFN can recover ground-truth counts in visual domains where previous pseudocount methods fail
Shows that CFN-based exploration bonuses lead to superior or competitive performance on challenging RL benchmarks, with improved robustness to stochasticity

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Exploration via Distributional Ensemble (EDE): encourages exploration of states with high epistemic uncertainty using an ensemble of Q-value distributions"],"problems":["Poor generalization of deep reinforcement learning (RL) agents in contextual Markov decision processes (CMDPs)","Neglect of exploration strategies in improving generalization in RL, with most prior work focusing on representation learning"],"datasets":["Procgen","Crafter"],"metrics":["Sample efficiency","Generalization performance (test performance on unseen environments)"],"results":[{"metric":"Generalization performance (Procgen and Crafter)","value":"EDE achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing strong policy-optimization and model-based baselines"},{"metric":"Sample efficiency","value":"EDE demonstrates significant improvement over naive exploration strategies"}],"novelty_claims":["First value-based RL method to achieve state-of-the-art generalization on both Procgen and Crafter benchmarks","Proposes a novel exploration method (EDE) that targets epistemic uncertainty via an ensemble of Q-value distributions","Demonstrates empirically and conceptually that exploration, not just representation learning, is crucial for generalization in RL"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Incorporating demonstrations into reinforcement learning (RL) to guide exploration","Auxiliary objective on demonstrations during RL training","Annealing the effect of demonstrations as the learned policy surpasses the demonstrator","Resetting from demonstration states to improve and speed up training","Building on Deep Deterministic Policy Gradients (DDPG) and Hindsight Experience Replay (HER)"],"problems":["Exploration in reinforcement learning with sparse rewards","Learning long-horizon, multi-step robotics tasks with continuous control","Overcoming the difficulty of random exploration in high-dimensional, complex environments"],"datasets":["Block stacking task in simulation with 100 human demonstrations collected via teleoperation in virtual reality"],"metrics":["Training speed (order of magnitude speedup)","Task success rate (solving block stacking tasks)","Comparison to baseline methods (RL alone, behavior cloning, initialization with demonstrations, fine-tuning with RL)"],"results":[{"metric":"Training speedup over RL","value":"Order of magnitude faster"},{"metric":"Task solvability","value":"Able to solve block stacking tasks not solvable by RL or behavior cloning alone"},{"metric":"Policy performance","value":"Often outperforms the demonstrator policy"}],"novelty_claims":["Demonstrates that using demonstrations with RL can solve complex tasks with difficult exploration that are not solvable by RL or imitation learning alone","Introduces a simple auxiliary objective on demonstrations and a method for annealing away demonstration influence as the policy improves","Proposes resetting from demonstration states to significantly improve and speed up training","Shows that the method outperforms commonly used alternatives such as initialization with demonstrations and fine-tuning with RL"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Curiosity in Hindsight (incorporating hindsight representations to disentangle noise from novelty in intrinsic reward calculation)","BYOL-Hindsight (instantiation of Curiosity in Hindsight for BYOL-Explore)"],"problems":["Exploration in sparse-reward or reward-free environments","Fragility of curiosity-driven exploration in stochastic environments (e.g., agents getting stuck in high-entropy/noisy states)","Distinguishing between novelty (predictable, reducible error) and noise (inherently unpredictable, irreducible error) in intrinsic motivation"],"datasets":["Grid world environments with various stochasticities","Atari games with sticky actions (including Montezuma's Revenge)"],"metrics":["Exploration performance (e.g., state coverage, score)","Robustness to stochasticity (performance under sticky actions)"],"results":[{"metric":"Exploration performance in Montezuma's Revenge with sticky actions","value":"State-of-the-art results (quantitative value not specified in provided text)"},{"metric":"Performance in non-sticky setting","value":"Performance preserved compared to BYOL-Explore (quantitative value not specified in provided text)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a method to disentangle noise from novelty in curiosity-driven exploration using hindsight representations derived from structural causal models.","Introduces Curiosity in Hindsight, a scalable generalization of curiosity that is robust to stochasticity.","Instantiates the framework in BYOL-Hindsight, demonstrating improved robustness to stochasticity and state-of-the-art exploration in challenging environments like Montezuma's Revenge with sticky actions."]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm based on the maximum entropy RL framework","Automatic gradient-based temperature tuning for entropy regularization"],"problems":["High sample complexity in model-free deep reinforcement learning","Brittleness to hyperparameters in deep RL algorithms, especially temperature parameter in maximum entropy RL","Stability and convergence challenges in off-policy RL with high-dimensional, nonlinear function approximation"],"datasets":["Benchmark RL tasks (e.g., MuJoCo locomotion tasks)","Real-world quadrupedal robot locomotion tasks","Robotic manipulation with a dexterous hand from image observations"],"metrics":["Sample efficiency (number of environment steps to reach a certain performance)","Asymptotic performance (final average return)","Stability across random seeds"],"results":[{"metric":"Sample efficiency","value":"SAC achieves state-of-the-art sample efficiency, outperforming prior on-policy and off-policy methods including TD3"},{"metric":"Asymptotic performance","value":"SAC achieves higher final average return than prior methods on benchmark and real-world tasks"},{"metric":"Stability across random seeds","value":"SAC achieves similar performance across different random seeds, indicating high stability"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), an off-policy actor-critic algorithm based on the maximum entropy RL framework","Development of an automatic temperature tuning method that eliminates the need for per-task hyperparameter tuning","Demonstration of SAC's superior sample efficiency, stability, and asymptotic performance compared to prior state-of-the-art RL algorithms on both benchmark and real-world robotic tasks"]}
